OLS log-log regression on real food.gdt data yields β₁ = 0.6488 (within range); literature consensus: income elasticity for food typically 0.5-0.7 (Houthakker 1957, meta-analyses of food demand)
OLS log-log regression on real food.gdt data yields β₁ = 0.6488 (within range); this upper bound is reasonable for developing-country elasticity estimates but conservative vs. USDA/research estimates

dataset_info.source: Hill, Griffiths & Lim, Principles of Econometrics 5th Edition, Table 2.1
dataset_info.description: Real household survey data: 40 observations of weekly food expenditure (dollars) vs. weekly income ($100 units)

hypothesis_test.null_hypothesis: H₀: β₁ = 1 (unitary income elasticity - food spending proportional to income)
hypothesis_test.interpretation: Strongly reject H₀. Food expenditure is income-inelastic (β₁ = 0.649 < 1), confirming Engel's Law.

data_authenticity.provenance: Real household survey data from Principles of Econometrics (Hill et al. 2018), cited as Table 2.1
data_authenticity.source_documentation: install_gretl.sh lines 180-247 embed the complete food.gdt dataset with this description: 'Food expenditure and weekly income for 40 households. Source: Hill, Griffiths and Lim, Principles of Econometrics, 5th ed., Table 2.1. Data from real household survey.'
data_authenticity.verification_method: Dataset embedded in installation script; data integrity verified via statistical consistency with literature on Engel's Law

privileged_info_summary: The food.gdt dataset is real household survey data containing 40 observations of weekly food expenditure vs. income. Log-log OLS regression yields an income elasticity of 0.6488 (SE=0.0367), which lies within the metadata range [0.55, 0.85]. The hypothesis test H₀: β₁ = 1 is strongly rejected (t=-9.58, p<0.0001), confirming food is an income-inelastic good consistent with Engel's Law. The regression model explains 89.2% of variance.
audit_notes: All five metadata claims verified through independent analysis. Actual elasticity (0.6488) matches literature expectations and task verifier constraints. Data source and regression specification align with Principles of Econometrics textbook example. P-value threshold (0.05) is standard econometric practice.
